subject: Chemistry
title: What Was the Philosopher's Stone?
description: A quick win: understand What Was the Philosopher's Stone?.

hook.text: The philosopher's stone wasn't actually a stone. It was supposedly a substance that could transmute lead into gold.

buildup.text: Alchemists believed a 'perfect' substance could perfect imperfect metals—turning base metals noble.

discovery.text: It was also called the 'elixir of life'—capable of curing all diseases and granting immortality.

twist.text: Some alchemists claimed it was a red powder. Others said a liquid. No two descriptions matched.

climax.text: Nuclear physics can turn lead into gold—remove three protons with a particle accelerator. It costs far more than gold.

punchline.text: Alchemists dreamed of turning lead to gold. Physics can do it—uneconomically.

quiz.question: Can lead actually be turned into gold?
options: Yes, by removing protons in a particle accelerator—but it costs far more than gold
No, it is physically impossible to change one element into another
Yes, certain bacteria can convert lead into gold naturally
